sound_type: soft whistles + mimicry -- the quiet corvid, gentle whistled notes, quiet chuckling, low mewing, and mimicry of other species
ecosystem: Subalpine forest -- mountain hemlock, subalpine fir, whitebark pine, Engelmann spruce. High-elevation conifer belt.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Breeds in late winter (February-March) -- earlier than almost any other songbird.

coordinates.description: Reference for subalpine forest habitat in the Cascades, Washington. Gray Jays found throughout subalpine forests of the Cascade Range, Olympic Mountains, and other high-elevation areas of the Pacific Northwest.

taxonomy_note.description: FIFTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-104) -- the Corvid Quintet. Perisoreus is a different genus from the previous corvids (Corvus for raven/crow, Cyanocitta for Steller's Jay). NEW GENUS in the engine. The Gray Jay was renamed Canada Jay by the American Ornithological Society in 2018, restoring the historic common name used before 1957.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Corvidae count: 6 (Steller's Jay 53, Raven 100, Raven pair 101, American Crow 102, Steller's Jay raucous 103, Gray Jay 104)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Perisoreus from Greek peri ('around') + soreuo ('to heap up') -- referring to the bird's food-caching behavior of heaping stored food. canadensis = 'of Canada.' Common name 'Gray Jay' is descriptive. 'Canada Jay' restored in 2018 by AOS. 'Whiskey jack' from Algonquin wisakedjak, a trickster spirit. 'Camp robber' from the bird's habit of stealing food from human campsites.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Perisoreus is new to the engine at degree 104. Three species worldwide: Gray Jay (P. canadensis, North America), Siberian Jay (P. infaustus, Eurasia), Sichuan Jay (P. internigrans, China). All are boreal/montane forest specialists with food-caching behavior.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes. Fifth consecutive corvid passerine at degrees 100-104.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae continues its unprecedented run. Five consecutive degrees (100-104) is the longest same-family consecutive run in the entire engine -- bird or mammal.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,000-4,000 Hz. Soft whistled notes are tonal and relatively narrow-band compared to the harsh broadband alarm of the Steller's Jay.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,500-3,000 Hz. Moderate frequency range. The soft whistles occupy a middle register -- higher than the raven's cronk, lower than the Steller's Jay's alarm.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Soft whistled notes: 1,500-3,500 Hz, tonal, gently modulated. Chuckling: broadband, low-frequency, quiet. Mewing: 1,000-2,500 Hz, nasal quality.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual whistle: 0.3-0.8 seconds. Chuckling series: 1-3 seconds. Vocalizations are generally brief and conversational.
song_metrics.note_structure: SOFT TONAL WHISTLE: The primary vocalization is a gentle, clear, whistled note -- sometimes ascending, sometimes descending, always soft relative to other corvids. No harsh quality. Conversational rather than declarative.
song_metrics.note_types: Soft whistle (primary, contact), chuckling (social, close range), mewing (nasal, longer range), mimicry (various species), alarm (harsher but still quieter than Steller's Jay). Repertoire is varied but consistently softer than other corvids.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Conversational. Whistles and chuckles combined in short, varied sequences. No fixed phrase structure. Context-dependent and socially modulated.
song_metrics.timbre: Soft. Clear. Gentle. Musical when whistling. Slightly nasal when mewing. The antithesis of the Steller's Jay's harsh rasp. The most approachable timbre in the Corvidae quintet.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- moderate energy. Audible within 50-100 meters. Not a long-range broadcaster. The quietest voice in the Corvidae quintet. The soft whistles are conversational -- meant for nearby birds and nearby humans.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate. Unhurried. The Gray Jay's vocalizations lack the urgency of the Steller's Jay's alarm or the authority of the raven's cronk. The tempo is conversational and relaxed.
song_metrics.context: SOCIAL AND CONVERSATIONAL. Contact calls between pair members and family groups. Food-related calls. Mimicry (less conspicuous than Steller's Jay). Alarm calls (softer than other corvids). The primary function is social cohesion within the family group.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Moderate. Some geographic variation documented. Cultural learning present but less studied than in ravens. Individual variation in mimicry repertoire.
song_metrics.key_property: SOFT WHISTLES + MIMICRY -- the quiet corvid. E=4. Gentle, clear, conversational. The friendliest corvid -- approaches humans, steals food (camp robber, whiskey jack). Food caching: thousands of items per year in tree bark using sticky saliva. Late-winter breeding. Subalpine forest. FIFTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-104). The Corvid Quintet. Forty-fourth consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Medium-sized, fluffy jay. Gray above, lighter gray to whitish below. Dark nape and partial dark cap. No crest. Round-headed, big-eyed appearance. Fluffy feathering for cold insulation.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 104: (1) GRAY overall with DARK NAPE -- dark cap extends from behind the eye across the nape. (2) WHITISH FOREHEAD and face. (3) NO CREST -- unlike Steller's Jay. (4) FLUFFY -- round-headed, soft appearance. (5) APPROACHABLE -- if it flies toward you in the subalpine forest, it is a Gray Jay.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round gray plumage. Juveniles are uniformly dark gray (almost charcoal) -- strikingly different from adults.
plumage.flight_pattern: Buoyant, slightly undulating flight. Glides frequently. Often flies to nearby perch rather than sustained flight. Will fly directly to humans carrying food.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized jay. Body length 25-33 cm. Wingspan 45 cm. Weight 62-82 g. Gray above, lighter below. Dark nape. White forehead. No crest. Fluffy, round-headed appearance.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 104: fluffy gray bird in subalpine forest that approaches you. Dark nape, white forehead, no crest. If it lands on your hand or steals your sandwich -- Gray Jay.
physical_description.field_id: Fluffy gray jay in subalpine or boreal forest. No crest (unlike Steller's Jay). Dark nape, white forehead. Approaches humans fearlessly. Soft whistled calls. If you are hiking above 4,000 feet in the Cascades and a gray bird lands near you and steals your trail mix -- it is a Gray Jay.

nodes.title: Soft Whistles + Mimicry -- The Quiet Corvid
nodes.description: Gentle, clear, conversational whistled notes. E=4. The quietest voice in the Corvidae quintet. Conversational rather than declarative. The whisper after the alarm. Contrasts with the raven's cronk, the crow's caw, and the Steller's Jay's shriek.

nodes.title: Fifth Consecutive Corvidae (100-104) -- The Corvid Quintet
nodes.description: Raven (100, deep cronk), Raven pair (101, duet), American Crow (102, sharp caw), Steller's Jay (103, raucous alarm), Gray Jay (104, soft whistles). Five degrees, one family. Longest same-family consecutive run in the entire engine. Five acoustic characters spanning E=4 to E=5.

nodes.title: The Camp Robber -- Human-Approaching Intelligence
nodes.description: The friendliest corvid in North America. Approaches humans without hesitation. Steals food from campsites, packs, and outstretched hands. 'Camp robber' and 'whiskey jack' (from Algonquin wisakedjak, trickster spirit). The boldness is intelligence -- the bird has learned that humans mean food and exploits that knowledge.

nodes.title: Food Caching -- Thousands of Items Per Year in Tree Bark
nodes.description: Stores thousands of food items per year by gluing them to tree bark with sticky saliva. Spatial memory allows relocation months later. Breeds in late winter (February-March) -- relies on cached food when the subalpine forest is still buried in snow. The caching strategy is survival architecture for the harshest environment any songbird endures year-round in the PNW.

nodes.title: Subalpine Forest -- Above the Steller's Jay, Below the Pika
nodes.description: Mountain hemlock, subalpine fir, whitebark pine. The Gray Jay occupies the high-elevation conifer belt above the Steller's Jay's lowland conifer territory (103) and below the American Pika's alpine talus (74). Shared subalpine habitat with the American Marten (65). Year-round resident at elevation. Does not migrate.

cross_references.relationship: CORVIDAE QUINTET (100-104). Steller's Jay in lowland conifer (E=5 alarm); Gray Jay in subalpine conifer (E=4 soft whistles). Complementary elevational niches. Maximum acoustic contrast: harshest corvid voice (103) followed by softest corvid voice (104).

CORVIDAE QUINTET (100-104). Intelligence. Both demonstrate food caching, problem-solving, and cultural learning. The raven caches in the ground; the Gray Jay caches in tree bark with sticky saliva. Different caching strategies, same cognitive foundation.

CORVIDAE QUINTET (100-104). Maximum habitat separation: crow in urban/open lowlands, Gray Jay in remote subalpine forest. The most and least human-adjacent habitats (though both species interact with humans).

SUBALPINE SHARED HABITAT. Both year-round residents of the high-elevation conifer belt. The marten is a predator of the subalpine; the Gray Jay is a prey species and competitor. Shared dependence on intact subalpine forest.

ALPINE NEIGHBOR. Pika in alpine talus above treeline; Gray Jay in subalpine forest below treeline. Adjacent elevational bands. Both adapted to extreme cold. Both cache food for winter -- the pika in hay piles, the Gray Jay in tree bark.
